items.title: Trump’s Tariffs Are Raking in Billions. Where Does It All Go? | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>President Trump repeatedly claims tariffs will reap “billions” of dollars for the U.S.—and so far, he’s right. According to the Penn Wharton Budget Model, the U.S. has collected more than $158 billion as of early September 2025. So where does that money go and how does Congress appropriate that money? 

WSJ examines how much money the U.S. has made from tariffs, how the money from tariffs is collected and where it ultimately goes. 

Chapters:
0:00 Tariff revenue 
0:36 How much the U.S. has made
2:16 How tariffs are collected
3:18 Where the money goes
4:38 Tariff legal challenges 

News Explainers
Some days the high-speed news cycle can bring more questions than answers. WSJ’s news explainers break down the day's biggest stories into bite-size pieces to help you make sense of the news.

#Trump #Tariffs #WSJ</article>

items.title: Charlie Kirk Shooting: A Look at His Rise as a Conservative Activist
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>WSJ’s Aaron Zitner explains how conservative activist Charlie Kirk’s approach to persuading people made him an important figure in Republican politics, particularly in President Trump's orbit.

#CharlieKirk #Politics #Shorts</article>

items.title: How Taco Bell’s CEO Keeps Gen Z Obsessed | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Taco Bell is seeing sales rise despite an overall slump in the fast food industry. The restaurant chain recently made a big bet on chicken amid increased competition in the space: in the last year, McDonald’s, Chipotle and Wendy’s all launched chicken-focused campaigns. So how does Taco Bell keep retaining customers and staying ahead of trends in a crowded space?

WSJ spoke with CEO Sean Tresvant about how he keeps people coming back even as they pull 
back on spending.

Chapters:
0:00 Taco Bell sales
0:27 How Taco Bel is succeeding right now
0:57 Chicken investments
3:16 Staying on trends
4:28 Attracting Gen Z
6:10 Value offering
7:50 Growth 

#TacoBell #Food #WSJ</article>

items.title: I Wore a Suit That Made Me Feel Like I Was 80
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>It took two people to make WSJ’s Amy Dockser Marcus feel like a woman in her 80s.⁠
⁠
At the MIT AgeLab, which works on finding ways to improve life for the elderly, researchers let her spend a day in their age-simulation suit. She hoped the experience might bring greater insight into what it’s really like to age—and how to prepare.⁠

#MIT #Aging #Shorts</article>

items.title: What China’s Military Parade Reveals About Its $3B Arms Exports | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>China’s military parade in the heart of Beijing allowed President Xi Jinping and the PLA to demonstrate the growing power of China’s defense industry. China sells billions of dollars worth of military equipment to more than 40 countries. But for Beijing, arms trade is not just a lucrative business.

WSJ examines how China’s move from one of the world’s biggest arms buyers into a weapons producer is strategic to gain influence and exercise global power. 

Chapters:
0:00 China’s military parade
0:38 China’s arms modernization
1:38 Arms trade
3:55 Global influence

News Explainers
Some days the high-speed news cycle can bring more questions than answers. WSJ’s news explainers break down the day's biggest stories into bite-size pieces to help you make sense of the news.

#China #Military #WSJ</article>

items.title: CDC Officials on Why the U.S. Is 'Incredibly Vulnerable Now' | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>The head of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention Susan Monarez was recently fired by the White House and several senior officials resigned in protest, fueling more concerns about the U.S. health policies under RFK Jr. 

Three former officials of the CDC, including recently resigned Chief Medical Officer Debra Houry, discuss the future of the agency and public health in the U.S. 

Chapters:
0:00 CDC turmoil, explained
0:37 Monarez’s removal
2:13 How the CDC works
3:25 What’s next for the CDC?
4:40 Potential solutions

#CDC #RFK #WSJ</article>

items.title: How Back-to-School Shopping Influences Retailers and Consumers
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Guest co-host Miriam Gottfried and Adrienne Yih, senior retail analyst at Barclays, are set to dive into the impact of tariffs on companies such as American Eagle Outfitters, Gap and Abercrombie & Fitch. Then, Yih tells Miriam why this back-to-school season is important for apparel retailers.

#Retail #AI #Shorts</article>

items.title: Hooters CEO on Pivot: No More ‘Butt Cheeks Hanging Out’ | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Hooters is remaking its brand image after filing for bankruptcy in March. For years, the casual restaurant chain has been operated by two different companies: one owned by private equity that emphasizes the brand’s sex appeal as a “breastaurant” and one that includes the original founders that says it’s actually “family friendly.” So how does Hooters plan to turn the brand around amid growing competition in the food industry?

WSJ sat down with Hooters Management Corporation CEO Neil Kiefer, who shared his turnaround plan.

Chapters:
0:00 Hooters’ bankruptcy
0:27 Where the brand stands now
3:16 Rebrand plan
5:48 Challenges and competition 

#Hooters #Food #WSJ</article>

items.title: When Should You Stop Financially Supporting Your Adult Children?
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Whether they plan for it or not, plenty of parents are likely to find themselves in the same boat. About 60% of parents with children ages 18 to 34 said they had helped their kids financially in the previous year, according to a 2024 Pew Research Center survey. Parents are finding that the rising expenses that trail them from their child’s birth through college are now extending well into adulthood.

They commonly chipped in for housing, debt payments and everyday expenses such as groceries, according to a Bankrate survey last year. A third of younger millennial home buyers got help with the down payment from friends or family, according to an April report from the National Association of Realtors.

#Parenting #Tips #Shorts</article>

items.title: Top Money-Saving Hacks for Raising Kids
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Parenting demands a heaping dose of creativity—to entertain your kids, to foster their learning, to get them to eat vegetables. Applying a bit of that creativity to saving money can chip away at the massive expense of raising kids. And it might actually be fun. WSJ asked parents with kids of all ages for their best money-saving hacks.

#Parenting #Budgeting #Shorts</article>

items.title: CA vs. TX: The Redistricting Arms Race to Control Congress | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>President Trump has a narrow majority in the U.S. House of Representatives and Texas’s redistricting plan aims to give the GOP five new seats. California Democrats and Gov. Gavin Newsom are planning on redistricting to counter Trump’s push, with other states like New York, Missouri and Florida also looking to capture more seats. So what’s at stake for Congress?  

Matthew Klein, an analyst at the non-partisan Cook Political Report, breaks down the proposed Texas and California redistricting maps for the 2026 midterm elections and what it means for the balance of power in Congress.

Chapters:
0:00 The redistricting battle
0:26 How Republicans are redistricting in TX
1:20 Houston area
2:02 South Texas area
2:27 Dallas-Fort Worth area
2:54 Austin area
3:35 California’s response
4:15 Northern California area
5:11 Other states responses

News Explainers
Some days the high-speed news cycle can bring more questions than answers. WSJ’s news explainers break down the day's biggest stories into bite-size pieces to help you make sense of the news.

#Congress #Texas #WSJ</article>

items.title: Why Timeshares Still Thrive Despite Their Scammy Reputation
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>The U.S. timeshare industry has consolidated behind a few major brands: Hilton Grand Vacations, Marriott Vacations Worldwide and Travel + Leisure. The industry has evolved significantly from its notoriously scammy origins, having much higher standards and using more flexible points-based systems. Executives say the bait-and-switch days are behind timeshares and have rebranded them as “vacation clubs,” offering a wide range of accommodations and experiences. But there are still issues and scams to look out for when getting into a timeshare. WSJ explores how timeshares became a $35.7 billion dollar industry, how they work and how issues still persist in the industry. 

#Travel #Timeshare #Shorts</article>

items.title: Why Russia Will Own and Operate Turkey’s First Nuclear Plant
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Russia is financing, building, and operating Turkey’s first nuclear plant—a $25 billion project. The Akkuyu Nuclear Power Plant is a big step towards energy independence and economic development. But for NATO, the new plant is a potential security risk as tensions continue to grow with Moscow. WSJ explains what the century-long partnerships between Russia and Turkey means for the region and the future of NATO.

#Russia #Turkey #Shorts</article>

items.title: How China’s $285M Export Hub in Brazil Will Cost U.S. Billions | WSJ Center Point
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>China’s new $285 million export terminal in Brazil, being built outside of São Paolo by Chinese state agricultural giant Cofco, threatens billions of dollars in revenue for U.S. farmers. This new terminal and Trump’s recent 50% tariffs on many Brazilian goods threaten to push China and Brazil even closer together. 

WSJ’s Samantha Pearson explains how this new terminal and growing ties between China and Brazil will continue to hurt U.S. farmers.

Chapters:
0:00 China’s new export terminal 
0:21 China-Brazil trade
1:14 Port of Santos
2:05 Trump’s tariffs and U.S. farmers’ losses
2:58 Other Chinese projects in Brazil
4:16 What's next?

Center Point
WSJ’s Center Point highlights points of interest pivotal to trade and industry— and the economic activity transforming them.

#Brazil #China #WSJ</article>

items.title: Crocs Is Crushing It in China. Here’s Why | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Crocs is thriving in China with the country’s Gen Z consumers as other U.S. brands like Nike, Apple and Starbucks are losing ground. Despite a weaker economy and geopolitical tensions with the U.S., Crocs’ revenue in China has grown more than 30% during the most recent quarter as a result of adapting to cater to Chinese consumers. 

WSJ’s Jon Emont headed to one of their main stores in Shanghai to find out how Crocs are winning and appealing to Gen Z.

Chapters:
0:00 Crocs growth in China
0:54 Targeting Gen Z
1:58 How Crocs catered to Chinese taste
3:43 Lessons for other U.S. brands

News Explainers
Some days the high-speed news cycle can bring more questions than answers. WSJ’s news explainers break down the day's biggest stories into bite-size pieces to help you make sense of the news.
